era_context: The early 12th century was characterised by the consolidation of the Crusader States in the Levant, the reign of Henry I in England and Normandy (following his accession in 1100), the Song Dynasty's continued dominance in China, and the fragmentation of the Seljuk Empire into regional sultanates. This was a period of significant cross-cultural contact, military conflict, and administrative development.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)

events.title: Crusaders capture Acre (Akko) from Muslim forces
events.description: The Crusaders, under the command of Baldwin I of Jerusalem and other Crusader leaders, captured the city of Acre (Akko) from Muslim forces. This was a significant military victory that expanded Crusader territorial control in the Levant and secured an important port city. The capture of Acre provided the Crusaders with a strategic stronghold on the Mediterranean coast and enhanced their ability to maintain supply lines and conduct further military operations. The city would remain a major Crusader stronghold for nearly two centuries.
key_figures: Baldwin I of Jerusalem (King of Jerusalem)

sources.name: Fulcher of Chartres, History of the Expedition to Jerusalem

events.certainty_note: The capture of Acre in 1104 is well-documented in multiple Crusade sources and Islamic chronicles. The date is consistently given as 1104 in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1099-001 (First Crusade and establishment of Crusader States)

events.title: Henry I of England continues consolidation of power; administrative reforms
events.description: Henry I of England, having secured his position through military victory and political maneuvering, continued to implement administrative reforms and strengthen royal authority. In 1104, he was engaged in establishing the legal and financial systems that would characterise his reign. He promoted the development of the royal court, strengthened the judiciary, and began the process of creating a more centralised administrative apparatus. These reforms would have lasting effects on English governance.
key_figures: Henry I of England (King of England and Duke of Normandy)

sources.name: William of Malmesbury, Gesta Regum Anglorum

events.certainty_note: Henry I's administrative reforms occurred over several years during his reign. Specific events in 1104 are less distinctly documented than the broader pattern of his governance.

events.title: Bohemond I of Antioch remains imprisoned by the Danishmends
events.description: Bohemond I, Prince of Antioch, remained in captivity with the Danishmend Turks, having been captured in 1100. During his imprisonment, his nephew Tancred served as regent of Antioch, managing the principality's affairs and conducting military campaigns. Bohemond's continued captivity affected the political dynamics of the Crusader States and demonstrated the ongoing military threat posed by Turkish forces in Anatolia.
events.certainty_note: Bohemond's imprisonment is well-documented. He was captured in 1100 and released in 1103, so the 1104 date requires correction. By 1104, he had already been released.

events.title: Song Dynasty continues military pressure against Jurchen Jin Dynasty
events.description: The Song Dynasty, under Emperor Huizong, continued to face military pressure from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty in the north. The Jin Dynasty, which had been established in Manchuria, posed an ongoing threat to Song territorial integrity. In 1104, the Song court was engaged in managing this military threat while maintaining the sophisticated administrative and cultural systems that characterised the dynasty. The military pressure from the north would eventually lead to the Jin conquest of northern China in the 1120s.
key_figures: Huizong of Song (Emperor of the Song Dynasty)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The Song Dynasty's military struggles with the Jurchen Jin Dynasty are well-documented in Chinese sources. Specific events in 1104 are less distinctly recorded, but the general context of military pressure is well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: Bohemond I was released from captivity in 1103, not 1104, so his continued imprisonment in 1104 is inaccurate. The capture of Acre in 1104 is well-documented and confirmed. However, some sources date the capture to 1103, creating a one-year discrepancy in the historical record. The significance of 1104 as a distinct year in English history is less pronounced than 1106 (Tinchebrai) or 1100 (Henry I's accession). The military pressure on the Song Dynasty from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty is well-documented, but the specific events of 1104 are not distinctly recorded.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1104 is dominated by Western sources, particularly Crusade chronicles and English administrative records. Islamic and Byzantine perspectives on events in the Levant exist but are less frequently consulted in English-language historiography. Chinese sources provide detailed administrative records but are less frequently consulted in Western historiography. The year 1104 appears to be a relatively significant year for Crusader military expansion (with the capture of Acre) but less significant for other regions. The bias toward military and political events over economic or social history is pronounced in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The capture of Acre in 1104 was part of the ongoing consolidation of the Crusader States established in 1099
The Crusader capture of Acre in 1104 established a stronghold that would be recaptured by Saladin in 1187
Bohemond's situation in 1104 was a consequence of his capture in 1100
